text.content: Enhancing educational evaluation through predictive student assessment modeling

title.plain_text: Enhancing educational evaluation through predictive student assessment modeling

tables.heading: Summary Table PRED
rows: 253 second and third year undergraduate students
5 Classes in Web Design, Web Technologies and Java Programming
LMS logs, Student Demographics, Academic Background

[Consistency, Following, Review Score, Attendance Score, Midterm Exam Score, Compulsory, Average Score in Other Subjects, Entrance Exam Score, Age, Gender, HighSchool GPA, HW Submission Rate, Subject]

Data was standardized and nans were imputted using kNN. 

Categorical data was One-Hot-Encoded
kNN, Random Forest, Logistic Regression, SVM, Naive Bayes, Stacking
Authors do a standard classification problem on data from an Online Platform. The goal is to predict end of course performance.

Models overfit.

Prediction of average grades would benefit from Midterms but authors intend to use it earlier.

Additional data sources could be explored.

There is no integration of other AI tools.

blocks.text: Abstract

text.content: Abstract

rich_text.plain_text: Abstract

blocks.text: The work uses an interactive LMS to collect data about students and use it to forecast success. Data sources involves interaction patterns, time on exercises and, finally, progress across learning activities. Authors use multiple classifiers, with a Stacking model being the better one at predicting grade rages.

text.content: The work uses an interactive LMS to collect data about students and use it to forecast success. Data sources involves interaction patterns, time on exercises and, finally, progress across learning activities. Authors use multiple classifiers, with a Stacking model being the better one at predicting grade rages.

blocks.text: Introduction

text.content: Introduction

rich_text.plain_text: Introduction

blocks.text: The rise of Covid increased the demand for digital ways to deliver educational content, which has contributed towards making the grading of students online somewhat of a nightmare, especially if the professors are not well versed in online evaluation.

text.content: The rise of Covid increased the demand for digital ways to deliver educational content, which has contributed towards making the grading of students online somewhat of a nightmare, especially if the professors are not well versed in online evaluation.

rich_text.plain_text: The rise of Covid increased the demand for digital ways to deliver educational content, which has contributed towards making the grading of students online somewhat of a nightmare, especially if the professors are not well versed in online evaluation.

blocks.text: In previous studies, authors have attempted to use different measurements to collect data “on the fly” and use it for predictive modelling, but most studies have been rather unsuccessful.

text.content: In previous studies, authors have attempted to use different measurements to collect data “on the fly” and use it for predictive modelling, but most studies have been rather unsuccessful.

rich_text.plain_text: In previous studies, authors have attempted to use different measurements to collect data “on the fly” and use it for predictive modelling, but most studies have been rather unsuccessful.

blocks.text: The aim of this paper seems to be to use “any means” to extrsct student data and use it for predictive modelling of student performance. The model is meant to explore different instructional conditions. RQ1 is aimed to verify whether it is possible to predict student performance from the start of the course. RQ2 explores which factors are the most likely to determine an outcome.

blocks.text: It is difficult to create an early warning system: The methods we use are often unreliable to detect students who may be struggling. The authors consistently contest methods used to assess what are effective predictors of student success:

blocks.text: Attendance has been found to positively impact performance but usually these observations result from a small sample and, more importantly, from the comparison between simple means.

blocks.text: Grades

rich_text.plain_text: Grades

blocks.text: Fernandes used GBoost on demographics and in-term grades and found that static attributes (the kind that is non-actionable) exhibit the highest predictive potential - gender, demographics. Jiang found quizzes and peer-assessments to be some of the most effective determiners of course-performance. Bernacki was able to only use LMS data to predict performance.

text.content: Fernandes used GBoost on demographics and in-term grades and found that static attributes (the kind that is non-actionable) exhibit the highest predictive potential - gender, demographics. Jiang found quizzes and peer-assessments to be some of the most effective determiners of course-performance. Bernacki was able to only use LMS data to predict performance.

rich_text.plain_text: Fernandes used GBoost on demographics and in-term grades and found that static attributes (the kind that is non-actionable) exhibit the highest predictive potential - gender, demographics. Jiang found quizzes and peer-assessments to be some of the most effective determiners of course-performance. Bernacki was able to only use LMS data to predict performance.

blocks.text: Something interesting:

text.content: Something interesting:

rich_text.plain_text: Something interesting:

blocks.text: Unlike Bernacki, these authors make a clear argument that including grades is paramount to predict performance.  For example, the authors fully accept a work that uses the midterm exam grades to predict the end-of-course grades (even though, more than likely, these grades are a part of the computation of the final grade). Well, at least they end the section acknowledging that the cited works only self-propagate already existing biases.

text.content: Unlike Bernacki, these authors make a clear argument that including grades is paramount to predict performance.  For example, the authors fully accept a work that uses the midterm exam grades to predict the end-of-course grades (even though, more than likely, these grades are a part of the computation of the final grade). Well, at least they end the section acknowledging that the cited works only self-propagate already existing biases.

For example, the authors fully accept a work that uses the midterm exam grades to predict the end-of-course grades (even though, more than likely, these grades are a part of the computation of the final grade). Well, at least they end the section acknowledging that the cited works only self-propagate already existing biases.

blocks.text: They also mention the use of a Multiple Features Fusion Attention Mechanism Enhanced Deep Knowledge Tracing (which I always understood as the task of predicting the answer to next question based on previous questions - not specifically something to measure student performance explicitly).

text.content: They also mention the use of a Multiple Features Fusion Attention Mechanism Enhanced Deep Knowledge Tracing (which I always understood as the task of predicting the answer to next question based on previous questions - not specifically something to measure student performance explicitly).

blocks.text: In general, this literature review makes a very broad and general broad-strokes look into “what is student performance and compiles multiple ways of going about it. There does not seem to a specific rule on what is the task at hand, or the data. It’s very confusing and awkward to read.

There does not seem to a specific rule on what is the task at hand, or the data. It’s very confusing and awkward to read.

blocks.text: Data: Authors use multiple features extracted from five different classes made available in VnCodelab. Students are allowed to interact with the platform during class. Actions by student and professor are captured in real time.

text.content: Data: Authors use multiple features extracted from five different classes made available in VnCodelab. Students are allowed to interact with the platform during class. Actions by student and professor are captured in real time.

rich_text.plain_text: Data: Authors use multiple features extracted from five different classes made available in VnCodelab. Students are allowed to interact with the platform during class. Actions by student and professor are captured in real time.

blocks.text: Data refers to 253 undergraduate students of Computer Science with similar demographics and backgrounds.  Authors collected demographic data and 2 main variables: total time dedicated in the system & time spent doing reviews outside regular class hours.  The system also includes other performance-related daqta - Grades, GPAs, etc…

blocks.text: Model Selection - Algorithms considered were RF, LR, SVM, NB and KNN. Stacking classifier uses all of them into a SVM meta-estimator. The task at hand is multi-class classification, where the goal is to predict a Grade Range.

blocks.text: Authors start by checking the performance of the models using only features that are directly used to compute the grades. Then, they exclusively use demographics, then followed by interaction with the course and (finally?) performance in events prior to current course/subject, whatever they are considering… I’m so confused…

blocks.text: The models were evaluated using classification metrics and other things that definitely are not metrics such as the “confusion matrix”. Also, how are these ROC scores being computed if we’re using multiclass classification? Are the scores seen the ovr averages? So many questions…

blocks.text: Comparam 5 base models vs um stacking dos 5 modelos. O melhor modelo aparenta ser o stacking por ser aquele que tem melhores resultados e, simultaneamente, não overfitta.  O melhor score utiliza 13 variáveis??? Confesso que nunca percebi muito bem qual é a experiência…

blocks.text: The authors start with the analysis of a correlation matrix between features. The explanation of color coding is incorrect. Attendance, Midterm Exam and averaqge grade on other subjects display the highest correlation with the Grade Range for the course.

text.content: The authors start with the analysis of a correlation matrix between features. The explanation of color coding is incorrect. Attendance, Midterm Exam and averaqge grade on other subjects display the highest correlation with the Grade Range for the course.

blocks.text: The authors also do a second level of analysis where they look to create different models by iteratively removing one of the features and then looking into the decline in predictive performance. By looking at both techniques, the authors find that Gender and Compulsory have close to no impact on Grade Range.

blocks.text: Discussion

text.content: Discussion

rich_text.plain_text: Discussion

blocks.text: The authors argue that they are proposing an EWS, which they definitely aren’t as their model uses, among other things, the Midterm Exam score. There is also no mention as to when they are making the prediction (i.e. they mention that they use data from 5 classes, is this the first 5 classes of a semester??). Their overall ML discussion of voerfitting is also innacurate.

text.content: The authors argue that they are proposing an EWS, which they definitely aren’t as their model uses, among other things, the Midterm Exam score. There is also no mention as to when they are making the prediction (i.e. they mention that they use data from 5 classes, is this the first 5 classes of a semester??). Their overall ML discussion of voerfitting is also innacurate.

Their overall ML discussion of voerfitting is also innacurate.

blocks.text: Prior academic performance is a predictor of student success, same with attendance, in course performance, etc… Gender does not seem to be a significant contributor. In fact, the authors answer RQ by explicitly mentioning that demographics seems to play no role on a sample where they themselves claim to be almost homogeneous).
